A lab claims a result came “not through scaling, but through architecture.” Taken literally, that slogan draws a boundary: the achievement is supposed to come from design rather than sheer size. But if the architecture depends on pretrained priors obtained by large-scale training elsewhere, then the boundary is porous. The visible system may be small or cleverly arranged, yet some of its competence is imported from scaling done upstream. So the claim is at least incomplete, and possibly misleading if heard as independence from scale.

Inside that lab, the model proposes explanations and sometimes marks one with language like “I have it.” That confidence does not necessarily track truth. By premise, it may overfit the narrative of discovery itself: candidates that sound like breakthroughs can be made to appear stronger than they are. So a judge reading the outputs faces two stacked distortions. First, the system’s apparent originality may partly be inherited from scaled priors. Second, its internal-seeming confidence may selectively amplify weak but dramatic answers.

Now add the judges. If they preferentially select outputs resembling canonical scientific creativity, then they reinforce both distortions. A candidate that looks like a familiar flash of insight and is presented with conviction will be easier to choose, even if its quality is mediocre. Meanwhile, an explanation that does not resemble human historical patterns of discovery—even if superior—risks being discarded as odd, opaque, or unserious. Selection then becomes less a search for best explanation than a filter for recognizable style.

That means the evaluation loop can systematically favor inherited priors plus polished performance over actual novelty. “Architecture” receives credit; scaled pretraining supplies much of the substrate; confidence language decorates fragile hypotheses; human taste finalizes the error.

The remaining premises show the same structure in miniature. “The vengeful crows presume the photos captured their ankle.” Whatever the photos actually contain, the crows infer a specific injury to themselves. That is a prior-driven interpretation, not neutral perception. “A delay in billing can make a quoted customer choke.” A quoted number may already strain a customer; if billing is delayed, the eventual charge can land with greater shock. In both cases, timing and framing shape reaction more than underlying reality.

So the world implied by all five premises is one where surface cues are repeatedly mistaken for substance. Credit is assigned to architecture despite hidden dependence on scaling. Confidence masquerades as discovery. Human judges prefer the familiar image of genius and suppress alien truth. Crows infer an ankle from a photo. Customers choke not just on price but on delayed presentation of it. Across domains, priors, narrative forms, and presentation effects dominate judgment.
